text: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of cosinor-based techniques tailored for time-series analysis in chronobiology. Framed as a regression problem, the method offers the distinct advantage of being applicable to non-equidistant data. A significant benefit of this approach is the ability to derive precise confidence intervals for rhythmic parameters with known periods, leveraging the least squares procedure and highlighting the value of incorporating prior external information. While originally designed to handle short and sparse datasets, the extended cosinor has evolved into a robust tool for analyzing long-term time series, facilitating both rhythm detection and parameter estimation. We examine the fundamental assumptions of the cosinor model, provide diagnostic tools to verify them, and present strategies for managing non-stationary data. Through various illustrative examples, we demonstrate the versatility of these methods, extending their application from traditional circadian rhythm research to the mapping of complex, broad-scale time structures known as chronomes.
